Q1: I tried to keep a consistent study routine, usually dedicating about 18 hours a week spread across different days. I'd typically study in the evenings after my part-time work, since that's when I could focus without worrying about other responsibilities. Being a read-write learner, I spent a lot of time making detailed notes and rewriting concepts in my own words. Sometimes the routine felt mechanical, but I pushed through because I knew I needed to stay on track despite everything else going on.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on deadlines and what was happening in my life. I tried to maintain regular study times, but with limited resources and needing to work, things would shift around constantly. Some weeks I was ahead, other weeks I was scrambling to catch up before due dates. The inconsistency bothered me because I knew I could do better if my circumstances were different.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew an assignment was coming up or when I needed to check on course announcements. Sometimes I'd log in just to watch the video lectures when I had a quiet moment at home. The discussion posts also prompted me to check in, since I tried to participate when I could. Honestly, a lot of my logins were driven by anxiety about missing something important.
Q4: After a few weeks, logging in became somewhat automatic, especially in the mornings before I started my day. I'd check for new materials or see if anyone responded to my discussion posts. It wasn't exactly enjoyable, but it became a habit I maintained. The routine helped me feel like I had some control over my academic life even when other things felt chaotic.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful to me because I could download them and study at my own pace. I also found the discussion forums helpful because they let me see how other students understood the material. The video lectures were good too, though I often had to watch them multiple times to really absorb everything. Written content just sticks better in my brain than anything else.
Q6: I tended to skip some of the optional supplementary videos and extra resources because I simply didn't have the time or energy. With seven online courses and limited resources, I had to prioritize what would directly impact my grades. Some peer interaction features felt awkward to use, so I only engaged minimally with those. I wish I could have explored more, but survival mode meant cutting corners somewhere.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could clearly see how it connected to assignments or exams. If I could tell it would help me understand a concept I was struggling with, I'd invest the effort. Practical exercises that reinforced the readings were especially valuable to me. Anything that felt like busywork without clear purpose got pushed to the bottom of my list.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford most of the time. Required activities had urgency attached to them, so they got done no matter what. With optional stuff, I'd think about doing it but then something else would come up and it would slip away. There was always this guilt about not doing more, but I had to be realistic about my capacity.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge portion of my studying happened offline with downloaded PDFs and handwritten notes. I'd print materials when I could access a printer and study from those during my commute or work breaks. I also used some free textbook resources I found online to supplement the course content. My LMS activity probably showed way less than the actual hours I put into learning.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes or re-reading printed materials for hours, but none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also study with a classmate occasionally, quizzing each other from our notes. Sometimes I'd spend an entire weekend working through concepts without touching the LMS at all. The system only captured a fraction of my actual engagement.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd download everything in one session, then work through it over several days offline. With unreliable internet at home sometimes, it was just more practical to have everything saved locally. I'd only go back online to submit work or check for updates. My single login might represent a whole week's worth of studying.
Q12: There were definitely moments when everything felt overwhelming, especially mid-semester when assignments from all seven courses piled up. Financial stress made it hard to concentrate, and I'd feel this heaviness that made even opening my laptop difficult. The sadness would creep in and I'd question why I was pushing so hard. Those were the times when my engagement dropped even though I desperately wanted to keep up.
Q13: Fatigue was a constant companion, honestly. Working to support myself while managing seven courses meant I was always running on empty. When I was exhausted, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities definitely meant the LMS got less attention than it probably should have. I had to make impossible choices about where to direct my limited energy.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing while my mind wandered to bills I needed to pay or shifts I needed to cover. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. The stress made it hard to be fully present even when I was technically engaged. I was there physically but mentally somewhere else entirely.
Q15: I postponed exam preparation a few times because the anxiety around it was paralyzing. I knew the exams were important, but facing them meant confronting how underprepared I felt. Sometimes I'd avoid difficult readings because I didn't have the mental energy to struggle through them. The avoidance made things worse, but in the moment it felt like the only way to cope.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd structure my week around what was due and when, prioritizing accordingly. As deadlines approached, my LMS activity would spike dramatically. Without deadlines, I might have drifted, so in a way they kept me anchored even though they also caused stress. Everything revolved around those dates.
Q17: When I got positive feedback on assignments, it gave me a small boost of motivation to keep going. Negative or critical feedback sometimes sent me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course for a few days. I tried to use feedback constructively, but with my low resilience, it often hit harder than it should have. The timing of feedback mattered too—late feedback felt less useful.
Q18: The course structure helped me know what to expect, which reduced some anxiety. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me a framework to organize my limited time around. However, when the structure was rigid without flexibility, it clashed with my unpredictable life circumstances. I appreciated knowing what was coming, even if I couldn't always meet every expectation.
Q19: The availability of downloadable resources made engagement much easier for someone in my situation. Clear instructions and well-organized modules helped me navigate efficiently. What made it harder was when participation requirements assumed everyone had equal time and resources. Mandatory synchronous sessions were particularly challenging with my work schedule.
Q20: I think those students might be genuinely trying but struggling to produce finished work. They could be reading, watching, and learning but hitting a wall when it comes to synthesis or writing. Maybe anxiety or perfectionism prevents them from submitting. Or perhaps life circumstances keep interrupting at the crucial moments when work needs to be finalized. High activity doesn't always mean high output capacity.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. I performed reasonably well despite what my LMS activity might suggest because so much of my learning happened offline. Students who are efficient with their time or who learn well independently might not need to be constantly logged in. The LMS captures presence but not necessarily the quality of learning happening elsewhere. Performance and logged activity aren't the same thing.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access, timing, and frequency pretty accurately. They capture when students are active online and what resources they click on. But they completely miss offline studying, emotional engagement, depth of understanding, and the quality of attention during logged sessions. They also miss the context—why someone's activity dropped or what external factors were at play.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm actively thinking about concepts even outside of study time, making connections to other things I know. True engagement feels like investment, like the learning matters to me personally. It's harder to achieve when you're stressed and stretched thin, but when it happens, it's meaningful.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally while still pushing through. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with printed notes on the bus or studying during work breaks. It can't measure the effort it took to stay motivated when everything felt heavy and sad. My engagement was often invisible—happening in the margins of a difficult life, sustained by sheer determination rather than ideal circumstances.
